California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
June 25, 1991
Diedre' D. Lingenfelter, clrc
city clerk
city of corona
815 west Sixth Street
P. O. Box 940
Corona, CA 91718
Re: 5::"li:T:: i:;'I;::'nar 
Assistance
Dear lls. Lingenfelter:
This is in response to your letter requesting advice on
behali of corona city councilrnember BiII Franklin regarding his
respons ibi I it ies under the conf l ict-of- interest provisions of the
pol'iticaL Reforn Act (the ,Act").1 
Since your advice request does
not refer to a specific governrnental decision but instead seeks
leneral guidance, we are treating your request as one for inforrnal
assistance.z
OUESTTON
May councilnember Franklin participate in city council
decisioirs which nay rnaterially affect ctients of his enployer on
whose projects the councilnember has not been involved?
CONCLUS TON
councilrnenber Franktin is required to disqualify himself from
any decision before the city council which would foreseeably and
materially affect any economic interest, including his enployer
1 cor"..,..nt code Sections 81ooo-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government code unless otherrrrise i,ndicated. Commission
regulations appear at 2 california code of Regulations Section
18ooo, et seo. A11 references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California code of Regulations.
2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor r.rith the
immunj.ty provided by an opinion or fornal written advice. (Section
83114; Regulation 18329 (c) (3) . )
^428 J Street, Suite 8ot-t . P.O. Box 8tr7 . Sacramento CA 95804-0807 . (916) 322-i66O


Our File No. I-91-262
Page 2
and clients on v/hose accounts he has worked, if the effect on the
econornic interest rrould be different from the effect on the public
generally.
FACTS
councilmember FrankLin is eurployed as a loan officer at a
mortgage company located in Riverside. The comPany works with
property throughout the western region of Riverside county. For
each sales transactj-on the councilmember grorks on, the
councilmenber and the broker receive a conmission. He receives no
comrnission for property transactions in which he does not
participate.
councilmember Franklin has become concerned that he has a
conf l-ict of interest with respect to any city council decj-sion
concerning any client of his employer. You stated that the
councilrnember has no access to information concerning transactions
in which he is not involved.
ANALYSI S
Section 87100 of the Act prohibits any Public official 
frout
naking, participating in naking, or otherwise using his.or her
official 
position to influence a governnental decision in whj.ch
the official 
has a financial interest. 
A 'rpublic of f iciaLt' is
defined in Section 82048 and Regulation 18700 (copy enclosed) as
every natural person who is a member, officer, enployee, or
consultant of a state or locaI government agency. This definition
vould include a menber of the Corona City Council.
Section 87103 specifies that a Public official 
has a
financial interest in a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable
that the decision wilI have a material financial effect,
distinguishable from the effect on the public AeneralIy, on the
official 
or a member of his or her imnediate family or oD:
(c) Any source of incone, other than gifts and
other than loans by a comrnercial lending institu-
tion in the regular course of business on terms
available to the public without regard to official
status, aggregating two hundred fifty dollars
($250) or more in value provided to, received by or
prornised to the public official within 12 nonths
prior to the tine when the decision is nade.
(d) Any business entity in which the public
official 
is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee, or holds any position of management.
Section 87103 (c) and (d).
Councilmember Franklin is a loan officer for a mortgage
company that does business in the jurisdiction. 
As an enployee of


Our Fi■e No. I-9■-262
Page 3
the company, the councilnember has an economic interest in the
company. uoreover, in the course of his ldork, the councilmember
will receive commission incorne for each real estate transaction i.n
which he participates.
cenerally, conmission incone is treated as incorne derived
frorn aII the participants in the transaction. Further, Regulation
18704.3 (copy enclosed) prov j.des:
(a) This section contains the disclosure and
disqual i f ication requirenents for any public
official 
who receives cornrnission income for
services rendered as an insurance broker or agent,
a real estate broker or agent, a travel agent or
salesperson, a stockbroker or a retail or wholesale
salesperson.
(b) r'Cornmission incone" neans gross payments
received as a result of services rendered as a
broker, agent, or other salesperson for a specific
sale or similar transaction. 
Conrnission incorne is
received when it is paid or credj.ted.
(c) The sources of comnission income in a
specific sale or simil.ar transaction include for
each of the fol lowing:
*rt
(2) A real estate broker:
(A) The person the broker
represents in the transaction;
(B) If the broker receives a
commission from a transaction conducted
by an agent working under the broker's
auspices, the person represented by the
agent ;
(c) Any brokerage business entity
through which the broker conducts
business; and
(D) Any person who receives a
finder's or other referral fee for
referring a party to the transaction to
the broker, or who tnakes a referral
pursuant to a contract with the broker.
***
(d) For purposes of deterrnining whether
disqualification is required under the provisions


Our File No. I-9■
-262
Page 4
of Sections 87100 and 87103(c), the full gross
value of any conmission incone for a specific sale
or similar transaction sha11 be attributed to each
source of incorne in that sale or transaction.
while not directly apPIicable, we can apPly Regulation
18704.3 by analogy. According to your facts the councilmenber
receives commission income for each real estate transaction he
participates in as a Loan officer. 
'Thus, according to the
-regulation, each cornmission the councilnember receives should be
attributed in ful] to both the councilnember's ernployer and the
client involved in the transactions for which the councilmenber
received a commission. other clients of the nortgage company
would not be economic interests of the councilnernber.
Thus, Councilrnernber Franklin is required to disqualify
himself from any decision before the city council which would
foreseeably and materially affect any econornic interest, including
his ernploylr and clients on whose accounts he has worked, if the
effect on the econornic interest nould be different fron the effect
on the public generally. 
(sectj'on 87103(c).)
Furthernore, these persons wilI remain econornic interests of
councilnember Franklin for Purposes of the confl ict-of- interest
provisions of the Act for 12 months after the commission incone
ias received by the council'menber. (section 87103(c).)
Therefore, for a period up to one year, Councilrnernber Franklin
also will be required to disqualify hirnself frorn participating in
any city council decision vhich r.riII have a foreseeable and
malerial financial effect on his enployer or clients on whose
accounts he has worked .
Foreseeable Material Financial Effects on Econornic Interests
whether the financial consequences of a decision are reason-
ably foreseeable at the tirne a governrnental decision is made
depends on the facts of each particular case. An effect is
considered reasonably foreseeable if there is a substantial
likelihood that it trilI occur. certainty is not required.
Houever, if an effect is only a rnere possibility, 
it is not
reasonably foreseeable. (In re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198,
copy enclosed. )
In addition, the effect on the counci lrnember' s economic
interest nust be rnaterial in order to require disqual if ication.
The commission has adopted differing guideJ.ines to deternine
whether an effect is material, depending on the specific
circumstances of each decision. For exanple, where the nortgage


Our Fi■e No. エー9■-262
Page 5
conpany is directly before the city council,3 "= .., applicant or
the subject of the decision, Regulation 18702.1(a) (copy enclosed)
provides that the effect of the decj.sion on the nortgage company
is deemed naterial and disgual if ication is reguired. 
(Conbs
Advice Letter, No. A-89-177, copy enclosed. )
where the econornic interest is not directly before the city
councif, but nay be indirectly affected, Regulations 18702.2 and
La702.6 (copies enclosed) app1y. Whether the indirect effect on a
business entity, such as the councilmenber's enployer is material
depends on the financial size of the business entity. 
For
exampte, Regulation La702.2 (g) provides that for a relatively
snall business entity, the indirect effect of a decision is
naterial where:
(1) The decision wil.I result in an increase
or decrease in the gross revenues for a fiscal year
of $10,000 or more; or
(2) The decision will result in the business
entity incurring or avoiding additional expenses or
reducing or elininating existing expenses for a
fiscal year in the anount of 52,500 or more; or
(3) The decision will result in the increase
or decrease in the vaLue of assets or liabilities
of S10, ooo or nore.
ltoreover, where the economic interest is a person and not
directly before the city council, such as a client for which the
city councilnember has worked, Regulation 18702.6 provides:
The effect of a decision is rnaterial as to an
individual who is a source of income or gifts to an
official 
if any of the following applies:
(a) The decision wilI affect the
individual's incorne, investhents, or other
tangible or intangible assets or liabilities
(other than real property) by Sf,000 or more;
or
' ,n9 mortgage company is considered directly before the city
council r.rhen the conpany initiates the proceeding by filing 
an
application, cIaim, appeal, or similar reguest, or is a named
party in, or the subject of, the proceeding, A person or business
entity is the subject of a proceeding if a decision involves the
issuance, renewal, approval, denial or revocation of any Iicense,
pernit, or other entitlernent to, or contract with, the subject
person or business entity. (Regulation I8702.1 (b).)


(b) The decision will affect the
individual's real property interest in a man-
ner that is considered naterial under section
:-8702.3 0r Section 18702.4.
where a financial, effect on an econornic interest will be both
foreseeable and material, the councilrnember may not participate.
with respect to any clients of the mortgage company for whom
the councilnember did not provide services, Councilmernber Franklin
rnay participate in decisions concerning such clients, provided the
aeliiion will not have a material financial effect on the mortqaoe
cornpanv, (See, Regulation l'8702.2.) Thus, the effect of a
aecision on other clients of the mortgage company should only be
considered to the extent that the effect on that client lrill
result in an increase or decrease in the gross revenues, the value
of the assets or liabilities, 
or expenses of the mortoaqe conpanv.
Effects on the other clients of the mortgaqe company which will
not affect the nortgaqe company itsetf do not result in a conflict
of interest.
Public GeneraIIv ExceDtion
Fina1ly, even if councilrnernber Franklin determines that he
has a financial interest that will be naterially affected by the
decision, he rnay still 
partici-pate in the decision if the effect
on the councilmember's financial interest is not distinguishable
from the effect on the public generally. For the 'rpublic
generally" exception to appIy, the decision must affect the source
of incorne in substantially the sane manner as it wil'I affect a
significant segment of corona. (Regulation 18703, copy enclosed. )
You have not provided facts concerning a specific decision so we
can only provide the general guidelines set forth above.
If you have any further questions regarding this natter,
please feel free to contact me at (916) 322-5901 .

-262
Page 6

scott Hallabrin
Acting General counsel
